definition: A set of rules or guidelines that define how a component should behave to be accessible.
A vertically stacked set of interactive headings that each reveal or hide a section of content.
Accessible Rich Internet Applications; a set of attributes that define ways to make web content and web applications more accessible.
A verifiable truth about a component's accessibility behavior or state.
The process of evaluating a component or application for accessibility compliance.
A custom focusable region that traps keyboard focus and supports keyboard navigation.
A category of interaction the system can use (e.g., keyboard, pointer).
The process of selecting the appropriate interaction strategy based on available capabilities.
A state that resolves differently depending on available capabilities.
A UI component that allows users to select one or more options from a set.
A composite widget combining a single-line textbox with a listbox, allowing users to select from a list or enter a custom value.
A reusable piece of UI, such as a menu, tab, or checkbox.
The degree to which a component or application meets accessibility standards.
A declarative specification of expected accessibility behavior for a UI component or pattern.
The process of running a contract against a component to validate its behavior.
The engine that executes contracts against a component to validate its behavior.
An execution that validates a component's deterministic accessibility behavior against a contract.
Meeting the requirements of accessibility standards such as WCAG.
The principle that a component produces consistent accessibility outcomes for the same interactions and conditions.
Domain-Specific Language; a fluent syntax for defining accessibility contracts and policies.
A user action, such as a click or key press, that can cause a state transition in a component.
A syntax style for defining contracts that reads like natural language, making it easier to write and understand.
The current element in the DOM that receives keyboard input.
The ability to move focus and interact with UI elements using the keyboard.
Text or element that describes the purpose of a form control or component.
A widget that presents a list of options from which the user can choose.
A format for defining contracts that can be parsed and executed by software.
A widget that offers a list of choices to the user, typically triggered by a button.
A recognizable UI structure (e.g. combobox, menu).
A UI component that allows users to select a single option from a set.
A selector that identifies an element based on its position relative to others in a collection (e.g., first, last, next).
An ARIA attribute that defines the type of an element for assistive technologies.
A selector targeting elements by ARIA role.
Software that reads the content of a screen aloud for users with visual impairments.
A mechanism used to identify elements within a component for interaction or validation.
Selectors that identify elements based on accessibility semantics such as roles, relationships, and structure rather than visual or CSS-based attributes.
A sequence of interactions used to bring a component into a specific state.
A named, self-contained description of a UI condition.
The process of building complex states from simpler dependent states.
A condition that must be satisfied for a state or assertion to be valid.
An assertion that is only valid within a specific state.
Accessibility validation that does not depend on interaction or state transitions.
A collection of states that define how to reach and verify UI conditions through setup and assertions.
A change from one state to another caused by an interaction.
A UI component that allows navigation between different panels of content.
A UI component that switches between two states, such as on/off.
A helper function or module that adds accessibility features to custom components.
The ease with which users can interact with a component or application.
Web Content Accessibility Guidelines; a set of recommendations for making web content more accessible.
